sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
NOW I see it! The frame is `g` (lowercase, light gray) and the divider is `G` (uppercase, gray). They're different colors!
So the big container frames are `g` (light gray), not `G` (gray). I've been clicking on `G` dividers but never on `g` frames!
Let me check the segmentation for `g` objects.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check for g (lowercase) objects in segmentation
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
for node in nodes:
    if node['color'] == 'g':
        print(f"Node {node['id']}: color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, boundary={node['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
There are two `g` objects:
- Node 9: g, 36 pixels, boundary [[18, 36], [18, 48], [24, 48], [24, 36]] - this is the top container frame (7x7 rectangle minus interior)
- Node 18: g, 36 pixels, boundary [[35, 21], [35, 27], [47, 27], [47, 21]] - this is the bottom container frame (13x7 rectangle minus interior)
I've NEVER clicked on these `g` frames! This could be the key. Let me try clicking on them.
For the top container frame, I can click on (18, 36) which is the top-left corner.
For the bottom container frame, I can click on (35, 21) which is the top-left corner.
Let me try clicking on the top container's g frame first.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking on the g frame of the top container at (18, 36)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 18, 'col': 36}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
